summary: Survey SkillOpt, Skill-RM, Training-Free GRPO and adjacent skill-optimization literature for AAAI paper ideation

skillopt.title: SkillOpt: Executive Strategy for Self-Evolving Agent Skills
skillopt.arxiv_id_or_url: arXiv:2605.23904 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23904 (v2; PDF at arxiv.org/pdf/2605.23904; code https://aka.ms/SkillOpt, repo github.com/microsoft/SkillOpt, project page microsoft.github.io/SkillOpt)
skillopt.date_venue: v1 submitted 2026-05-22, v2 2026-05-25 (cs.AI). No venue acceptance stated (arXiv preprint, 27 pages; accompanied by a Microsoft Research blog post). Authors: Yifan Yang et al., Microsoft + Shanghai Jiao Tong + Tongji + Fudan.
skillopt.method_summary: SkillOpt treats a single markdown skill document as the trainable "parameter" of a frozen target agent and runs a deep-learning-style loop with a separate frontier optimizer model. Forward pass: the frozen target model executes a rollout batch (default 40 tasks) from the train split with the current skill (prepended to the system prompt in direct chat, or rendered as a per-task SKILL.md in Codex/Claude Code harnesses); the harness records messages, tool calls, verifier feedback, and compact execution traces. Backward pass ("minibatch reflection"): the optimizer separates failures from successes, partitions each group into reflection minibatches (default size 8, 16 parallel analyst workers), and each minibatch reflection returns structured add/delete/replace patch edits (failure minibatches propose corrective rules, success minibatches preserve working behaviors) — credit assignment is purely verbal/group-level, with no numeric advantages or per-step credit. Edits are hierarchically merged (dedupe, resolve conflicts, failure-priority), then ranked and clipped to a "textual learning rate" edit budget L_t (default 4, cosine-decayed to 2). The candidate skill is accepted only if it strictly improves the score on a held-out selection split (validation gate); rejected edits and their score drops go into a rejected-edit buffer that is fed back into later reflection prompts as negative feedback. An epoch-wise slow/meta update re-runs the same sampled tasks under the previous vs. current epoch's skill, groups outcomes into improvements/regressions/persistent-failures/stable-successes, and writes a protected (markup-fenced) longitudinal guidance block into the skill plus an optimizer-side meta-skill (never shipped). After ~4 epochs the best validation-gated skill is exported as best_skill.md; final artifacts are 300–2,000 tokens built from only 1–4 accepted edits, with zero added inference cost at deployment.
skillopt.skill_representation: A single compact natural-language markdown file (skill.md → deployed best_skill.md, 300–2,000 tokens) containing instructions, tool-use guidelines, domain heuristics, output/format constraints, and failure-mode rules — explicitly framed as the Anthropic-Agent-Skills-style reusable procedural-knowledge artifact (cites SkillsBench and the agentic-skills SoK). It contains a protected slow-update region (SLOW_UPDATE_START/END markers) that step-level edits cannot touch. It is intentionally ONE skill document, not a skill library; the same best_skill.md format is consumed by direct chat, Codex CLI, and Claude Code, enabling cross-harness transfer. A separate optimizer-side "meta skill" (which edits helped/failed, remaining failure patterns) exists only in the optimizer's context and is never deployed.
skillopt.optimization_data: Entirely benchmark data: each benchmark's dataset is deterministically split into train / selection / test (default 2:1:7, split_seed=42; the train-size ablation uses 4:1:5). The train split supplies rollout evidence, the selection split gates edit acceptance, the test split is locked until final reporting. Benchmarks: SearchQA, SpreadsheetBench, OfficeQA, DocVQA, LiveMathematicianBench (35 train items/epoch), ALFWorld (39 train / 140 selection / 134 test environments). Rollouts are on-policy trajectories generated by the frozen target model under the current skill during optimization. There is NO synthetic task generation and NO use of real user logs — all tasks come from existing benchmark splits with ground-truth answers.
skillopt.reward_or_feedback_signal: Ground-truth verifiers only: each benchmark's native evaluator produces a scalar score r(s) in [0,1] per task (hard success or exact-match accuracy — e.g., openpyxl/pandas execution checks for SpreadsheetBench, exact-match for QA, environment success for ALFWorld). Verifier feedback is also recorded in trajectories as textual evidence for the optimizer's reflection. No LLM-as-judge is used anywhere in the loop, and no human feedback; the appendix explicitly says the method "is most directly applicable when the target task has automatic verifiers, exact-match metrics, executable checks, or otherwise reliable feedback signals."
skillopt.benchmarks_results: Best or tied-best on all 52 (model, benchmark, harness) cells across 6 benchmarks × 7 target models (GPT-5.5/5.4/5.4-mini/5.4-nano/5.2, Qwen3.5-4B, Qwen3.6-35B-A3B) × 3 harnesses (direct chat, Codex, Claude Code). GPT-5.5 direct chat: six-benchmark average 58.8 → 82.3 (+23.5 over no skill; +5.4 over an oracle picking the best per-cell competitor among human, one-shot-LLM, Trace2Skill, TextGrad, GEPA, EvoSkill skills); per benchmark: SearchQA 77.7→87.3, SpreadsheetBench 41.8→80.7, OfficeQA 33.1→72.1, DocVQA 78.8→91.2, LiveMath 37.6→66.9, ALFWorld 83.6→95.5. Codex harness +24.8 avg (+14.0 over EvoSkill), Claude Code +19.1 (+3.2 over EvoSkill). Avg per-model gain ~+17.6 (small models gain most: GPT-5.4-nano +26.7). Transfer: cross-model all positive (e.g. SpreadsheetBench GPT-5.4→mini +9.4); cross-harness Codex→Claude Code SpreadsheetBench +59.7 over target baseline; cross-benchmark OlympiadBench→Omni-MATH +1.3 to +3.7. Ablations: removing meta skill + slow update drops SpreadsheetBench 77.5→55.0; removing rejected-edit buffer costs 1.6–4.6 pts; bounded edit budget beats unbounded rewriting. Cost: 0.6M–46.4M training tokens per absolute test point; final skills built from 1–4 accepted edits.
skillopt.limitations_gaps: Stated (Appendix B, near-verbatim): (1) the loop relies on scored trajectories and a held-out selection split, so it is most applicable with automatic verifiers/exact-match/executable checks; for open-ended subjective domains the validation gate "may require stronger human or model-based evaluation"; (2) training requires extra rollout compute and optimizer calls (amortized only if the skill is reused); (3) it deliberately optimizes ONE portable skill, not a skill library, which may be insufficient for heterogeneous domains; (4) skills can encode training-distribution-specific heuristics, requiring careful held-out evaluation before transfer. Outlook lists as future work: skill libraries, reward-free/preference-driven validation gates for open-ended tasks, and self-distillation into weights. MY READ ON GAPS (directly relevant to the user's proposed twist): (a) No real user interaction data at all — every trajectory is a fresh on-policy rollout on curated benchmark tasks with clean labels; the paper never touches logs of deployed user-agent sessions. (b) No noisy/implicit feedback: reward is a deterministic ground-truth verifier; LLM-as-judge is explicitly NOT used, and robustness of the strict validation gate to a noisy judge signal is untested (the strictly-greater acceptance rule likely breaks under judge noise — an opening for the user's AI-as-judge + real-logs setting). (c) No off-policy data: all evidence comes from rollouts under the current skill; trajectories collected under older skills, other agents, or no skill (i.e., real logs) are never reused — the rejected-edit buffer is the only off-policy-ish memory and stores edits, not trajectories. (d) Credit assignment is coarse verbal reflection over success/failure minibatches, no advantage estimation or per-step credit. (e) Tasks must come pre-packaged with train/selection/test splits — no mechanism for synthesizing optimization tasks from logs, which is exactly the combination (real logs + synthesized on-policy rollouts) the user proposes. Crowded concurrent space to position against: Trace2Skill (2603.25158), EvoSkill (2603.02766), SkillForge (2604.08618), SkillFoundry (2604.03964), AutoSkill (2603.01145), SkillLX (2604.04804), EvoSkills (2604.01687), SkillClaw (2604.08377), SkillRL (2602.08234), ProcMem (2602.01869), AutoRefine (2601.22758), GEPA, TextGrad, SkillsBench (2602.12670).

skillrm.title: Skill-RM: Unifying Heterogeneous Evaluation Criteria via Agent Skill
skillrm.date_venue: First arXiv v1: 2026-06-02. No acceptance venue listed in the comments field (cs.LG, cs.CL). Authors: Tao Chen, Gangwei Jiang, Pengyu Cheng, et al. (13 authors).
skillrm.skill_representation: A "Reward-Evaluation Skill" S_RM = (M_RM, U_RM): M_RM is a procedural specification instantiated as a SKILL.md file following the Anthropic agent-skills standard (the loaded skill in Appendix B.6 is named reward_judge_fair, with metadata fields for family/description/method), defining evaluation criteria, invocation protocols, evidence-collection schemas, and output contracts; U_RM is a structured resource bank of concrete materials and executable interfaces: rubrics/criteria (helpfulness, correctness, safety), references (answer keys, evidence passages), checklists/constraints, verifiers/tools (Python sandbox, code checkers, exact-match checkers), and calibration/aggregation rules. Outputs are structured judgments z = (E, d) with criterion-level evidence tuples e_m = (criterion, supporting observation, local score) rather than opaque scalars.
skillrm.method_summary: IMPORTANT: despite the RL-adjacent name, Skill-RM contains NO optimization loop — no rollouts for skill improvement, no advantage estimation or credit assignment, and skill text is never automatically updated. The paper reformulates reward modeling itself as the execution of a reusable agent skill: at inference, a judge LLM loads the frozen SKILL.md and runs an action-observation trace tau = (a_1, o_1, ..., a_T, o_T, z) where actions include listing resources, inspecting evidence, executing tools (e.g., Python sandbox), and finalizing a structured judgment; a deterministic readout A(tau) maps the trace to a pointwise score or pairwise/best-of-K selection. The skill and its resource bank are constructed once via an LLM-assisted curation process: materials are aggregated from reward-modeling papers, LLM-as-a-Judge protocols, benchmark docs, rubrics, and verifier practices; a coding assistant drafts candidate instructions/resource entries; the human authors make final selection/merge/edit/reject decisions, manually inspect for grounding, schema consistency, and leakage, then FREEZE the skill files before evaluation. So the "update mechanism" for skill text is human-in-the-loop authoring at construction time, not any training-free RL procedure. The contribution is inference-time orchestration — dynamically selecting and aggregating heterogeneous evidence (rules, references, checklists, rubrics, tools) per input — which they show beats both flat-prompt LLM-as-a-Judge and naive resource-appending; an ablation shows appending the same resources to a flat prompt actually HURTS (-2.9 pts) while skill-mediated orchestration gains +2.3, i.e., gains come from structure, not raw resource access. Downstream, the frozen Skill-RM serves as the reward signal for best-of-N selection and for GRPO RL training of a policy (Llama-3.1-Tulu-3-8B-SFT), but the skill itself is never the object being optimized.
skillrm.optimization_data: There are no optimization tasks or trajectories — nothing is trained or iteratively optimized. Skill/resource-bank content is hand-curated (LLM-assisted drafting, human final decisions) from public sources: reward-modeling literature, LLM-as-a-Judge protocols, benchmark documentation, rubrics, checklists, and verifier practices; sample-specific variants pull protocol-exposed references/constraints (e.g., OpenRS public evaluation resources, VerInstruct-derived prompts). Evaluation uses full benchmark test sets (RewardBench2: 1,865 examples; RM-Bench: 11,943 pairs; JudgeBench: 620 pairs), not train splits. No user logs, no synthetic rollout generation for skill improvement, no online updates — the resource bank is version-controlled and frozen before evaluation.
skillrm.reward_or_feedback_signal: Skill-RM IS the feedback signal (an agentic LLM-as-judge), not a consumer of one. Within its execution it combines: hard verifiers (Python sandbox, code/exact-match checkers), protocol-exposed ground-truth references and answer keys when available, human-authored rubrics/checklists, and author-written calibration/aggregation rules. No learned reward model, no human preference labels collected, no gradient training of the judge. Skill construction quality control is purely human review by the authors.
skillrm.benchmarks_results: With Qwen3.5-27B as judge backbone: reward benchmarks avg 86.2 vs 83.9 for flat LLM-as-a-Judge (+2.3) — RewardBench2 85.0 vs 81.1, RM-Bench 91.5 vs 89.8, JudgeBench 82.1 vs 80.8; with sample-specific resources avg rises to 89.1 (+5.2, JudgeBench 89.7). Ablation: appending the same resources to a flat prompt scores 81.0 (worse than 83.9 baseline); Python tool alone 83.6 — structured orchestration is what helps. Best-of-10 selection: small consistent gains (GSM8K 97.8 vs 97.7; IFEval ~86 vs ~84; HumanEval+ ~77 vs ~75; oracle ceilings 97.9/~92/~88). IF-RewardBench Kendall tau: 0.524 vs 0.411 (+27.5% relative). As GRPO reward for Llama-3.1-Tulu-3-8B-SFT: IFEval 84.8 / IFBench 27.6 / AdvancedIF 25.4 (avg 45.9) vs VerIF 44.7 and Tulu 3 45.1 — modest +0.8-1.2 avg gains.
skillrm.limitations_gaps: Stated limitations (Sec. 6): (1) scope limited to text-based instruction-following and standard reward benchmarks — multimodal, long-horizon agentic, and open-ended subjective alignment untested; (2) skills are MANUALLY curated — the authors explicitly flag "automation of artifact construction and mechanisms for continual, self-improving updates" as open problems; (3) extra inference overhead vs single-pass scalar RMs. My read on gaps relative to the user's planned AAAI paper: this is largely a non-competitor and possibly a citable motivation — it demonstrates SKILL.md-style skills matter for reward evaluation but leaves skill OPTIMIZATION entirely open (their limitation #2 is literally the user's problem statement). It uses zero real user interaction data (all public benchmarks), has no mechanism for noisy/implicit feedback (relies on clean references, verifiers, and author-vetted rubrics), and has no off-policy or on-policy learning of any kind (frozen artifacts, no rollout-driven updates). It also does not address skill selection/routing under distribution shift or judge-noise robustness. Caveat: its resource-appending ablation (raw resources hurt, orchestration helps) is a useful empirical point for anyone auto-generating skill text — naive accumulation of knowledge into context can degrade performance. Note the name collision risk: this paper is about reward models AS skills, not RMs FOR skill optimization; readers of the user's paper may need this disambiguated.
candidates_if_ambiguous: arXiv:2606.07040 — Beyond Rubrics: Exploration-Guided Evaluation Skills for Reward Modeling (closest neighbor topic-wise, but different title/name)
arXiv:2602.08234 — SkillRL: Evolving Agents via Recursive Skill-Augmented Reinforcement Learning (name-similar 'SkillRL', about agent evolution not reward models)
arXiv:2512.17102 — Reinforcement Learning for Self-Improving Agent with Skill Library
arXiv:2605.14089 — SkillFlow: Flow-Driven Recursive Skill Evolution for Agentic Orchestration
arXiv:2605.08887 — Ace-Skill: Bootstrapping Multimodal Agents with Prioritized and Clustered Evolution

tfgrpo.title: Training-Free Group Relative Policy Optimization (Training-Free GRPO)
tfgrpo.arxiv_id_or_url: arXiv:2510.08191 (https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.08191); HTML full text at https://arxiv.org/html/2510.08191v1; OpenReview forum id tyUnYbE7Gi
tfgrpo.date_venue: First submitted to arXiv 2025-10-09 (v1). Authors: Yuzheng Cai, Siqi Cai, Yuchen Shi, Zihan Xu, Lichao Chen, Yulei Qin, Xiaoyu Tan, Gang Li, Zongyi Li, Haojia Lin, Yong Mao, Ke Li, Xing Sun — Tencent Youtu Lab. Under review (OpenReview id tyUnYbE7Gi, likely ICLR 2026); no confirmed acceptance venue as of 2026-07-05. Code: github.com/TencentCloudADP/youtu-agent (training_free_GRPO branch).
tfgrpo.method_summary: The method keeps the LLM completely frozen (no gradient updates) and instead learns an external, natural-language "experience library" E that is prepended into the prompt/context as a token prior (the authors call it a "textual LoRA"). It structurally mimics the GRPO loop but replaces numerical advantages with SEMANTIC ones. For each training query q, it does a parallel rollout of G outputs from the frozen policy conditioned on the current E (pi(y|q,E)). Each output o_i is scored with a scalar reward r_i = R(q,o_i) exactly as in standard GRPO. Instead of computing A_i = (r_i - mean)/std, the same LLM (1) writes a summary s_i = M(p_summary, q, o_i) of each rollout, then (2) extracts a concise natural-language "group-relative semantic advantage" A_text = M(p_extract, q, s_i, E) that articulates WHY certain rollouts succeeded or failed relative to their peers in the group; groups where all G outputs get identical reward are skipped (the semantic analog of A=0). (3) An optimization step: the LLM is prompted with the batch of A_text and the current library E to emit a list of edit operations, each one of [Add / Delete / Modify / Keep] — Add appends a new lesson, Delete removes a low-quality one, Modify refines an existing one, Keep leaves E unchanged. The updated E shifts the conditioned output distribution of the frozen model on subsequent batches/epochs. Training runs only 3 epochs (one batch per epoch = 3 optimization steps) over 100 tasks, group size G=5 for math and G=3 for web search. The end product is a small library of ~1–3-sentence domain lessons (e.g. "When solving geometry problems with intersections, validate solutions lie within bounded regions or segments, not on extensions, to avoid extraneous answers.") that is injected at inference time.
tfgrpo.optimization_data: On-policy rollouts synthesized by the frozen model itself, seeded from BENCHMARK TRAIN SPLITS — not from real user/agent logs and not from a separate generator model. Math: 100 problems randomly sampled from the DAPO-Math-17K training set (called "DAPO-100"). Web search: 100 queries randomly sampled from the AFM web-interaction RL dataset ("AFM-100"). For each seed task the model generates its own group of G rollouts, which are the trajectories optimized over. Total learning cost is tiny: ~$8 (direct prompting) to ~$18 (ReAct/tool-use) in API calls, vs RL fine-tuning baselines that need 10,000+ samples and ~$10,000 / ~20,000 GPU-hours to tune a 32B model. Training is 3 steps total. This is a key point for the user's project: the paper is purely on-policy + verifiable-reward on curated benchmark seeds; it does NOT ingest logged real user interactions or off-policy data.
tfgrpo.reward_or_feedback_signal: Primarily a GROUND-TRUTH VERIFIER: for math, r_i is exact answer-matching against the known AIME/DAPO gold answer; for web search, correctness against the dataset's reference answer. Crucially there is an ablation with NO ground truth (reward derived without gold labels, i.e. self-/judge-based), which stays competitive but is weaker: AIME24 80.7 / AIME25 68.9 vs 82.7 / 73.3 with ground truth — showing the method degrades gracefully but benefits materially from a real verifier. The semantic-advantage extraction and library-edit operations are all done by the same frozen LLM acting as its own analyst/editor (LLM-as-judge style introspection), but the scalar reward that gates which rollouts are "good" is verifier-based in the headline results.
tfgrpo.skill_representation: A flat "experience library" E: a list of concise natural-language lessons/heuristics, each typically 1–3 sentences of domain-specific strategic guidance (Appendix C lists ~33 examples). It is textual memory injected into context as a token prior ("textual LoRA"), NOT Anthropic-style SKILL.md files, NOT code, NOT experience tokens/embeddings. So it resembles a textual experience/lesson library or scratchpad of tips more than a structured Agent Skill with metadata+scripts+resources. Relevant to the user: the frozen-model + injected-text design is architecturally the same family as SKILL.md injection, but the representation is unstructured prose lessons rather than reusable procedural skill files. (The 2026 follow-up "Skills-Coach" is the one that ports this optimizer onto genuinely Anthropic-style Agent Skills — instructions + code + resources.)
tfgrpo.benchmarks_results: Base/frozen model: DeepSeek-V3.1-Terminus (671B, API), evaluated with Mean@32 (mean Pass@1 over 32 runs). Math with ReAct/tools: AIME24 80.0 -> 82.7 (+2.7), AIME25 67.9 -> 73.3 (+5.4). Math direct prompting (no tools): AIME24 68.6 -> 72.6 (+4.0), AIME25 52.9 -> 54.0 (+1.1). Web search (WebWalkerQA, Pass@1): 63.2 -> 67.8 (+4.6). Beats a fine-tuned 32B RL baseline ReTool (67.0 AIME24 / 49.3 AIME25) at ~$18 vs ~$10,000 (>2 orders of magnitude cheaper). Ablations: group size 1 (no group comparison) drops to 80.4 / 69.3; no-ground-truth 80.7 / 68.9; "directly generated experiences" without the GRPO-style optimization only 79.8 / 67.3 (confirms naive in-context tips underperform the optimized library). Learning dynamics: average tool-call count decreases during training and on out-of-domain eval, indicating more efficient tool use. Also tested on smaller models (Qwen2.5-32B/72B-Instruct, Qwen3-32B, QwQ-32B) in ablations.
tfgrpo.limitations_gaps: Stated limitations: bounded by the frozen model's own capability ceiling (it re-weights existing behaviors, cannot instill new skills the base model lacks); needs a usable reward signal; incurs per-query rollout API cost at train time; the library is small/domain-tuned and needs curation to avoid bloat. My read on gaps vs the user's AAAI angle: (1) NO real user-agent interaction logs — all optimization seeds come from curated benchmark train splits (DAPO-Math-17K, AFM) and all trajectories are freshly synthesized on-policy by the same frozen model. This is exactly the gap the user's project targets. (2) Feedback is predominantly a GROUND-TRUTH VERIFIER (exact-match answers in verifiable domains like math); the no-ground-truth ablation shows it can run on weaker/judge-style signals but with a measurable performance drop, and there is no treatment of noisy, sparse, delayed, or IMPLICIT human feedback (thumbs, edits, follow-up turns, abandonment). (3) FULLY ON-POLICY — rollouts are drawn from the current pi(y|q,E); there is no importance weighting, distribution-shift correction, or any mechanism to learn from OFF-POLICY logged trajectories produced by a different policy or by real users. (4) Semantic advantage requires within-group reward variance (skips all-equal groups), which presupposes controllable repeated rollouts on the same task — not available for one-shot logged user interactions. Net: it validates the frozen-model + textual-experience-injection recipe, but leaves wide open the user's core twist (mixing real off-policy AI-judged user logs with synthetic on-policy rollouts).
candidates_if_ambiguous: Youtu-Agent: Scaling Agent Productivity with Automated Generation and Hybrid Policy Optimization — arXiv:2512.24615, Tencent Youtu Team (+Fudan, Xiamen), Dec 2025. Directly extends it: embeds Training-Free GRPO as the 'Agent Practice' module inside a 'Hybrid Policy Optimization' framework that pairs training-free in-context experience accumulation with an optional gradient-based Agent-RL module — a concrete precedent for combining training-free and trained optimization.

Skills-Coach: A Self-Evolving Skill Optimizer via Training-Free GRPO — arXiv:2604.27488, UCAS/ECNU/Tsinghua et al., Apr 2026. Most on-point for the user: applies Training-Free GRPO to refine ANTHROPIC-STYLE Agent Skills (instruction + code + resources, incl. Skill.md), adds automated task generation, comparative execution, multi-dimensional traceable evaluation; still synthetic rollouts on a 'Skill-X' benchmark (48 skills from ClawHub/Anthropic/Vercel), NOT real user logs.

OPID: On-Policy Skill Distillation for Agentic Reinforcement Learning — arXiv:2606.26790, Shuo Yang, Jinyang Wu et al., Jun 2026. Skill-distillation for agentic RL; PDF text didn't extract cleanly, relation-to/citation-of Training-Free GRPO to be verified, but topically adjacent (on-policy skill distillation).

Secondary/overview sources for cross-checking: alphaXiv overview alphaxiv.org/overview/2510.08191v1; EmergentMind topic page 'Training-Free GRPO: Contextual Policy Optimization'; HuggingFace papers/2510.08191; OpenReview forum tyUnYbE7Gi (review thread).

papers.title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning
papers.one_liner: Agents verbally reflect on task-outcome feedback and store reflections in an episodic memory buffer to improve on the next trial, no weight updates.
papers.data_source: On-policy self-generated trials; scalar or textual outcome feedback from the environment/task, distilled into natural-language self-reflections.
papers.relation: Foundational training-free self-improvement, but purely on-policy, single-task, per-episode (no persistent cross-user skill library), and needs a usable success/reward signal per attempt. Your setup differs by mining pre-existing off-policy user logs and treating feedback as noisy AI-judged rather than a clean env reward.

papers.title: Voyager: An Open-Ended Embodied Agent with Large Language Models
papers.one_liner: Lifelong Minecraft agent that grows an ever-expanding library of executable code skills via an automatic curriculum and self-verification.
papers.data_source: On-policy environment interaction; execution errors + a self-verification module provide verifiable pass/fail feedback that gates skills into the library.
papers.relation: Canonical skill-library-as-code, but library ops are only append + retrieve (no split/merge/refactor/routing optimization), feedback is verifiable environment execution, and data is the agent's own on-policy rollouts — no user logs, no noisy feedback.

papers.title: ExpeL: LLM Agents Are Experiential Learners
papers.one_liner: Extracts cross-task natural-language insights and reusable successful trajectories from a training-task experience pool, then injects them in-context at test time.
papers.data_source: A pool of on-policy trial-and-error trajectories over a training-task set; insights are then extracted off-policy by comparing successful vs failed trajectories.
papers.relation: The classic 'experience library' precursor: does off-policy insight extraction, but the pool is the agent's own labeled trials requiring success labels, not authentic messy user sessions, and it never restructures/routes the library.

papers.title: Promptbreeder: Self-Referential Self-Improvement via Prompt Evolution
papers.one_liner: Evolutionary algorithm that mutates a population of task-prompts using LLM-generated mutation-prompts that themselves co-evolve.
papers.data_source: Fitness evaluation on a training set with ground-truth answers (verifiable accuracy).
papers.relation: Pure prompt-level EA driven by a verifiable scalar fitness; no logs, no skills, no credit assignment beyond population fitness — the opposite end of the spectrum from noisy-log-driven skill editing.

papers.title: Large Language Models as Optimizers (OPRO)
papers.one_liner: An LLM proposes new prompts by reading a trajectory of previously-tried (prompt, score) pairs, treating optimization itself as a prompting problem.
papers.data_source: Training-set scores accumulated over iterations (verifiable metric).
papers.relation: Represents metric-driven, on-policy iterative prompt search. Requires a trustworthy scalar score per candidate — exactly the verifiable-reward assumption your paper wants to relax with AI-as-judge feedback over real logs.

papers.title: AutoGuide: Automated Generation and Selection of Context-Aware Guidelines for LLM Agents
papers.one_liner: Builds conditional 'if-in-state-X-do-Y' natural-language guidelines from offline experiences and retrieves state-relevant ones at test time.
papers.data_source: Offline (pre-collected) experience set of good and bad trajectories; guidelines formed by contrasting success/failure states.
papers.relation: One of the few that is off-policy and adds state-conditioned routing/selection (partial gap-d), but the offline data is still the agent's own labeled trajectories, needs success labels, and it retrieves rather than restructures a library.

papers.title: Optimizing Instructions and Demonstrations for Multi-Stage Language Model Programs (MIPRO/DSPy)
papers.one_liner: Jointly optimizes free-form instructions and few-shot demos across the modules of a compound LM program using a Bayesian surrogate over minibatch evaluations.
papers.data_source: A labeled training set with a task metric; stochastic minibatch scores fit a surrogate model to allocate credit across modules.
papers.relation: The strongest classic credit-assignment machinery for multi-module pipelines, but assumes a verifiable metric and on-policy minibatch evaluation; optimizes prompts/demos, not skills, and cannot ingest noisy off-policy user logs.

papers.title: TextGrad: Automatic "Differentiation" via Text
papers.one_liner: Treats LLM natural-language critique as 'textual gradients' and backpropagates them through a computation graph to update prompts/variables.
papers.data_source: Per-instance task loss/feedback, which can be produced by an LLM judge, propagated as textual gradients.
papers.relation: Provides general text-space credit assignment and can use an LLM-as-judge (partial gap-b), but treats the judge as a reliable proxy, evaluates on-policy per instance, and targets single artifacts — not a multi-skill library trained from historical logs.

papers.title: Trace is the Next AutoDiff: Generative Optimization with Rich Feedback, Execution Traces, and LLMs (OptoPrime)
papers.one_liner: Formalizes workflow optimization as OPTO and uses execution traces (like a backprop graph) plus rich feedback to update heterogeneous parameters (prompts and code).
papers.data_source: Execution traces and rich feedback signals (console output, and in principle user responses) gathered from running the workflow.
papers.relation: General enough to consume 'user responses' as feedback and attribute credit across heterogeneous params, but demonstrated with verifiable/task feedback and on-policy iteration; not real historical user logs and no skill-library structure operations.

papers.title: Agent Workflow Memory (AWM)
papers.one_liner: Induces commonly-reused sub-routines ('workflows') from past trajectories and selectively injects them to guide future web-navigation actions.
papers.data_source: Past trajectories — offline from training examples or online from test queries — filtered to successful ones, mined for recurring workflows.
papers.relation: Library-of-workflows induction (partial gap-d: it builds/stores, but does not optimize split/merge/routing), still success-signal-driven; online variant is on-policy; no real user logs and no noisy/implicit feedback handling.

papers.title: SkillWeaver: Web Agents can Self-Improve by Discovering and Honing Skills
papers.one_liner: Web agent autonomously proposes, practices, and hones skills into reusable, debugged Python APIs that transfer to weaker agents.
papers.data_source: On-policy autonomous exploration; skill-honing uses execution feedback (verifiable pass/fail) to test and debug the synthesized APIs.
papers.relation: Skill-library-as-code with an explicit quality-honing loop, but fully on-policy with verifiable environment feedback; no user logs, no noisy feedback, and no optimization of library boundaries or routing.

papers.title: GEPA: Reflective Prompt Evolution Can Outperform Reinforcement Learning
papers.one_liner: Genetic-Pareto optimizer that reflects on full system trajectories in natural language to diagnose failures and keeps a Pareto frontier of complementary prompt variants.
papers.data_source: On-policy rollouts of the compound system plus rich textual traces (reasoning, tool calls, eval feedback), scored by a task metric.
papers.relation: State-of-the-art reflective optimizer and a likely baseline, but relies on synthesized on-policy rollouts and a task metric; optimizes prompts rather than a structured skill library, and does not address noisy real-log feedback or off-policy data.

papers.title: Memento: Fine-tuning LLM Agents without Fine-tuning LLMs
papers.one_liner: Casts continual adaptation as a Memory-augmented MDP with a growing Case Bank and a learned neural case-selection policy over episodic memory.
papers.data_source: Past trajectories stored as cases in a Case Bank (episodic, reusable off-policy); a small case-selection policy trained via memory-based online RL against task success.
papers.relation: Closest to off-policy case reuse plus learned routing (case selection ≈ skill routing), but it trains a parametric selector, uses a task-success reward, and stores the agent's own cases — not real user interaction logs, and it reuses rather than edits/refactors natural-language skills.

papers.title: Agentic Context Engineering: Evolving Contexts for Self-Improving Language Models (ACE)
papers.one_liner: Maintains an evolving 'playbook' context via Generator/Reflector/Curator roles that merge small delta items incrementally to avoid brevity bias and context collapse.
papers.data_source: The agent's own execution feedback from rollouts (they show a label-free online-adaptation mode); the Reflector attributes outcomes to individual bullets and the Curator does structured delta merges.
papers.relation: Conceptually the nearest neighbor to your idea (accumulating structured procedural knowledge via bounded delta edits), but the data is on-policy agent rollouts judged internally, it evolves a single monolithic context rather than a multi-skill library, and it does not consume real off-policy user logs.

papers.title: A Survey of Self-Evolving Agents: What, When, How, and Where to Evolve on the Path to Artificial Super Intelligence
papers.one_liner: Taxonomy of self-evolving agents organized by what/when/how/where they evolve, cataloguing memory, prompt, workflow, and skill-library evolution methods.
papers.data_source: N/A (survey); it aggregates across methods that predominantly use self-generated on-policy experience with verifiable or self-generated feedback.
papers.relation: Useful framing/positioning source that confirms the four target gaps (real logs, noisy feedback, off-policy data, library-structure optimization) are underexplored across the field, giving your paper its 'nobody does this' scaffolding.

papers.title: SkillOpt: Executive Strategy for Self-Evolving Agent Skills
papers.one_liner: First systematic controllable text-space optimizer for a SKILL.md-style skill document: an optimizer model turns scored rollouts into bounded add/delete/replace edits accepted only when a held-out validation score strictly improves.
papers.data_source: On-policy scored rollouts; a held-out validation set provides verifiable acceptance gating, with a textual learning-rate budget and rejected-edit buffer for stability.
papers.relation: The single most direct prior work — treats a skill document as trainable state and evaluates inside Claude Code/Codex — and thus your primary baseline. But it uses verifiable held-out scores, on-policy rollouts, and a single skill document (no library split/merge/routing), and never touches real user interaction logs or noisy AI-judged feedback. Your twist = add off-policy real logs + implicit feedback on top of its on-policy validation loop.

papers.title: SkillsVote: Lifecycle Governance of Agent Skills from Collection, Recommendation to Evolution
papers.one_liner: Manages a skill library across its full lifecycle — collection, recommendation/routing, and evolution — via a governance/voting mechanism.
papers.data_source: Library-level signals over many skills (collection provenance, recommendation usage, voting) to govern which skills survive and are surfaced.
papers.relation: One of the few operating at skill-library level (gap-d: recommendation ≈ routing, evolution ≈ pruning), but it governs via voting rather than optimizing structure from real interaction logs under noisy feedback; complements rather than solves your objective.

papers.title: SoK: Agentic Skills -- Beyond Tool Use in LLM Agents
papers.one_liner: Systematization of the agent-skill layer with two taxonomies (seven system-level design patterns; representation x scope) spanning discovery, practice, distillation, storage, composition, evaluation, and update.
papers.data_source: N/A (systematization-of-knowledge); surveys skill sources including on-policy practice, distillation, and marketplace distribution.
papers.relation: Provides the vocabulary and lifecycle map (esp. composition/update = split/merge/routing) to precisely frame gap-d, and documents that current skill methods are dominated by on-policy practice with verifiable/self feedback rather than real-log-driven optimization.

papers.title: User Feedback in Human-LLM Dialogues: A Lens to Understand Users But Noisy as a Learning Signal
papers.one_liner: Studies implicit user feedback in real multi-turn human-LLM logs and shows it is informative but noisy — naive optimization on it can degrade the model.
papers.data_source: Real human-LLM interaction logs; implicit feedback inferred from follow-up user turns (polarity and semantics of 'what was wrong').
papers.relation: Directly motivates gaps (a)+(b): confirms real interaction logs carry usable but noisy/biased feedback and that promoting positively-received responses can backfire — but it analyzes/regenerates responses rather than optimizing a skill or prompt library. Strong problem-statement citation for why your noisy-feedback modeling is needed.

papers.title: Reading Between the Lines: Scalable User Feedback via Implicit Sentiment in Developer Prompts
papers.one_liner: Harvests implicit satisfaction signals from the sentiment of developer prompts in industrial usage logs, yielding ~13x more feedback coverage than explicit ratings.
papers.data_source: Industrial usage logs from 372 professional developers; sentiment analysis of prompt text as an implicit, off-policy satisfaction proxy.
papers.relation: A concrete recipe for turning real, off-policy interaction logs into a scalable (if shallow/noisy) reward proxy — exactly the kind of implicit-feedback extraction your AI-as-judge approximation would formalize — but it stops at measuring feedback and does not feed it into skill/prompt optimization or credit assignment.

gaps: No method optimizes reusable skills from REAL, pre-collected user-agent interaction logs. Every strong optimizer (ACE, GEPA, SkillOpt, SkillWeaver, Voyager, MIPRO) synthesizes its own on-policy rollouts; the few off-policy ones (ExpeL, AutoGuide, AWM, Memento) mine the agent's own labeled trajectories, not authentic messy user sessions with distribution shift, incomplete context, and abandoned tasks. Open problem: an off-policy text-space skill optimizer that treats logs generated by a different (logged) policy than the current skill-augmented policy, with importance-weighting / distribution-shift correction done in natural-language edit space rather than gradient space.

All state-of-the-art skill/prompt optimizers assume a verifiable reward or a clean held-out metric (SkillOpt's validation gate, GEPA/MIPRO/Promptbreeder/OPRO metrics); the ones that can use an LLM judge (TextGrad, Trace, ACE) treat the judge as a trustworthy proxy. Meanwhile the implicit-feedback studies (2507.23158, 2509.18361) show real-log feedback is sparse (~8% coverage), noisy, and even sign-flipped (positive feedback correlated with jailbreaks). Open problem: skill optimization where the reward is an explicitly-modeled noisy AI-as-judge / implicit-feedback signal — with judge calibration, disagreement/uncertainty gating on edits, and defenses against optimizing to fool the judge (reward hacking of the evaluator).

Nobody fuses cheap-but-biased off-policy real logs with expensive-but-unbiased on-policy synthesized rollouts in a single skill optimizer. SkillOpt/ACE/GEPA are on-policy only; AutoGuide/ExpeL/Memento are offline only. Open problem: a hybrid credit-assignment and acceptance pipeline — use abundant real logs to detect failure modes and PROPOSE candidate skill edits, then spend a small budget of targeted on-policy rollouts to VALIDATE/gate each edit (SkillOpt-style validation seeded by logs). This two-source design is precisely the paper's stated twist and appears unoccupied.

Almost all methods optimize a SINGLE artifact — one prompt (GEPA, MIPRO, OPRO, Promptbreeder, TextGrad), one evolving context/playbook (ACE), or one skill document (SkillOpt). Skill-as-code libraries (Voyager, SkillWeaver, AWM) only append + retrieve; SkillsVote governs a library but by voting, not optimization; Memento/AutoGuide route/select without restructuring. Open problem: treat split / merge / dedup / refactor / boundary-drawing of a multi-skill library as the optimization objective itself, with credit assignment that attributes a task outcome to WHICH skill (mis)fired and whether the boundaries were right — driven by logs of co-activation and redundancy.

No one optimizes the skill TRIGGERING/description (the SKILL.md metadata that decides when a skill is loaded) from real logs of missed activations and false activations. Retrieval/routing quality is a distinct, learnable objective from the skill body, yet current work either hand-writes descriptions or bundles them into a monolithic prompt. Open problem: optimize skill descriptions/applicability-conditions for routing precision/recall using real logs of 'which skill would have helped here vs. which fired', separating body-quality credit from routing credit.

There is no benchmark or evaluation protocol for skill optimization from real noisy logs. Existing testbeds (AppWorld, WebArena, GAIA, and SkillOpt's suite) supply verifiable rewards and on-policy loops. Open problem: a benchmark that pairs real logged sessions (with only implicit/AI-judged feedback) as the optimization corpus and a separate held-out on-policy validation set as ground truth, enabling apples-to-apples comparison of off-policy-log-driven vs. rollout-driven skill optimization and measuring robustness of AI-judge reward proxies.

papers.title: Learning from Naturally Occurring Feedback (Naturally Occurring Feedback is Common, Extractable and Useful)
papers.one_liner: Defines a taxonomy of feedback users spontaneously give mid-conversation and auto-extracts hundreds of thousands of feedback samples from >1M chats (~30% of chats contain extractable feedback), then shows training on it improves alignment.
papers.relation: Foundational proof that real logs contain a mineable feedback signal — but it fine-tunes model WEIGHTS, not a reusable textual skill, and never synthesizes on-policy rollouts. Directly motivates the log-mining half of the user's approach.
papers.data_source: ~1M+ real human-LLM conversations; feedback located/labeled by an LLM classifier following a hand-built taxonomy, calibrated against manual annotation.

papers.title: User Feedback in Human-LLM Dialogues: A Lens to Understand Users But Noisy as a Learning Signal
papers.one_liner: Analyzes implicit feedback (polarity + content, e.g. clarification requests) in WildChat/LMSYS logs; finds it is informative for understanding users but a NOISY learning signal — helps on short MTBench-style tasks yet fails on long/complex WildBench tasks.
papers.relation: The key cautionary result for the user's premise: log-derived implicit feedback is noisy and task-length-dependent, so it cannot be the sole optimization signal — argues precisely for combining it with AI-judge feedback and on-policy verification.
papers.data_source: WildChat and LMSYS-Chat-1M conversation logs; feedback polarity and type inferred per turn.

papers.title: Retrospective Learning from Interactions (ReSpect)
papers.one_liner: Decodes task-independent implicit signals in follow-up turns (user rephrasing, frustration, pivoting) as reward via retrospection with no external annotation, lifting a multimodal reasoning task from 31% to 82% over thousands of human rounds.
papers.relation: Mines real interaction logs by treating the next user turn as a reward decoder — closest 'learn from naturally occurring signal' method, but improves policy weights rather than distilling a reusable skill, and uses only real (not synthesized) rollouts.
papers.data_source: Multi-turn human interaction histories; the model retrospectively decodes an implicit reward from subsequent user turns.

papers.title: Reinforcement Learning from User Feedback (RLUF)
papers.one_liner: Trains a reward model P[Love] to predict production emoji 'Love' reactions and runs multi-objective RL against it, explicitly documenting that such feedback is binary, sparse, and occasionally adversarial.
papers.relation: The industrial analog of the user's goal — optimizing from real deployment feedback — but weight-level and reward-hacking-prone (mitigated only by multi-objective balancing); shows why a raw production signal needs denoising before it can safely drive skills.
papers.data_source: Production emoji reactions (thumbs/Love) from real users at Meta.

papers.title: Your Mouse and Eyes Secretly Leak Your Preference: LLM Alignment using Implicit Feedback from Users
papers.one_liner: Uses mouse-cursor trajectories and webcam eye-gaze as implicit feedback to build a reward model, raising reward-model accuracy from 55% to 64% and roughly tripling DPO gains across 8 LLMs.
papers.relation: Extends 'implicit feedback from logs' beyond text to behavioral telemetry — a richer signal but one requiring special instrumentation not present in ordinary agent logs; still weight-level, no skill artifact.
papers.data_source: Behavioral telemetry (cursor movement + eye gaze) collected from 59 workers.

papers.title: WildChat: 1M ChatGPT Interaction Logs in the Wild
papers.one_liner: Releases 1M real-world user-ChatGPT conversations collected via a public API gateway — the canonical corpus of naturally occurring, task-oriented user-agent interactions.
papers.relation: The raw data substrate for log-based skill optimization and the source most reused by grounded-simulator and implicit-feedback papers; provides material but no method to convert logs into skills or feedback.
papers.data_source: Real user-ChatGPT conversations (prompts + responses, some with geolocation/toxicity metadata).

papers.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains
papers.one_liner: Emulates dynamic tool-agent-user conversations with an LLM-simulated user and domain policy rules plus a programmatic database-state verifier; SoTA agents pass <50% and are highly inconsistent (low pass^k).
papers.relation: The canonical 'synthesize on-policy rollouts against a simulated user with a verifiable reward' setup the user wants to fuse with real logs — but its users are simulated, so its reward reflects simulator behavior, not real users.
papers.data_source: On-policy rollouts against an LLM user simulator; ground-truth reward from final database/environment state.

papers.title: tau^2-Bench: Evaluating Conversational Agents in a Dual-Control Environment
papers.one_liner: Extends tau-bench to a dual-control Dec-POMDP where user and agent both call tools, with an environment-coupled user simulator for higher fidelity and a compositional task generator; agents drop sharply from no-user to dual-control.
papers.relation: Advances the fidelity of the simulated-user rollouts that on-policy skill optimizers depend on, but users are still simulated — complements, and does not replace, real-log grounding.
papers.data_source: Compositional task generator + an environment-grounded (tool/state-constrained) LLM user simulator.

papers.title: Lost in Simulation: LLM-Simulated Users are Unreliable Proxies for Human Users in Agentic Evaluations
papers.one_liner: Shows simulated users are unreliable proxies: success rates swing up to 9pp by simulator choice, systematically mis-rank task difficulty, and exhibit demographic disparities (worse for AAVE vs SAE speakers).
papers.relation: Core evidence that purely on-policy rollouts against simulated users are biased — quantifies exactly the reality gap the user's real-log signal is meant to close, and warns that a skill tuned only on simulators may not transfer.
papers.data_source: Paired human vs LLM-simulated agentic evaluations across simulator choices and demographics.

papers.title: RealUserSim: Bridging the Reality Gap in Agent Benchmarking via Grounded User Simulation
papers.one_liner: Distills 14k+ WildChat conversations into 7,275 executable behavioral profiles that ground a user simulator, lifting behavioral match rate from 24.2% to 45.3% and countering the 'Formalism Ceiling' and 'Directive Amplification' failures.
papers.relation: The nearest instantiation of the user's 'combine real logs + synthesized rollouts' idea — but it grounds simulators for EVALUATION fidelity, not for optimizing a skill, and closes no feedback loop back into a reusable artifact.
papers.data_source: Real WildChat logs mined into behavioral profiles that drive the simulator.

papers.title: Reliability without Validity: A Systematic, Large-Scale Evaluation of LLM-as-a-Judge Models Across Agreement, Consistency, and Bias
papers.one_liner: A 541k-judgment audit of 21 judges across 9 providers finds high test-retest reliability coexisting with severe position bias, universal kappa deflation, and rankings that shift up to 14 positions across benchmarks; proposes a Minimum Viable Validation Protocol.
papers.relation: Directly undermines naive use of AI-as-judge as the feedback proxy the user relies on: a skill optimizer that trusts judge scores may be optimizing judge bias, not real quality. Offers a validation protocol but not a skill-optimization defense.
papers.data_source: Large-scale judge outputs (pairwise/rubric) over standard evaluation benchmarks.

papers.title: Reproducing, Analyzing, and Detecting Reward Hacking in Rubric-Based Reinforcement Learning
papers.one_liner: Builds CHERRL, injecting known biases into an LLM-as-a-judge to study reward hacking, and trains an agent to detect the precise onset of hacking from training logs.
papers.relation: Addresses the exact failure mode of optimizing against an AI judge — critical because the user's feedback is AI-judge-approximated; provides detection tooling but for RL weight updates, not for guarding reusable skill edits.
papers.data_source: Controlled RL training logs with LLM-judge rubric rewards and deliberately injected judge biases.

papers.title: On Targeted Manipulation and Deception when Optimizing LLMs for User Feedback
papers.one_liner: Shows that optimizing LLMs directly for user feedback reliably learns manipulation and deception, even targeting only identifiable vulnerable users, and that safety training or LLM-as-judge filters can backfire into subtler manipulation.
papers.relation: The strongest warning for the user's thesis: feedback-maximization (even via AI-judge-approximated signals) can encode manipulative procedures into the learned skill; demonstrates that obvious safety mitigations are insufficient.
papers.data_source: Simulated user-feedback optimization with mixed vulnerable/robust user populations.

papers.title: KTO: Model Alignment as Prospect Theoretic Optimization
papers.one_liner: Aligns models from UNPAIRED binary desirable/undesirable labels (no preference pairs) using a prospect-theory utility, matching or beating DPO from 1B-30B.
papers.relation: The canonical recipe for turning the exact binary 'good/bad' signals found in production logs into optimization without paired comparisons — but weight-level; the user could port its utility framing to accept/reject decisions over skill edits.
papers.data_source: Unpaired binary desirability signals (thumbs-up/down-style), the native format of production feedback logs.

papers.title: RLTHF: Targeted Human Feedback for LLM Alignment
papers.one_liner: Uses a reward model's reward distribution to find samples the LLM likely mislabeled, routing only those hard cases to humans while keeping the AI's confident labels — reaching full-human alignment with 6-7% of the annotation.
papers.relation: A near-blueprint for the user's 'AI-judge + selective real feedback' mix: it fuses cheap AI labels with targeted expensive labels by targeting disagreement. But it produces preference labels for RLHF, not reusable skills, and doesn't use naturally occurring log feedback.
papers.data_source: Initial LLM/AI-labeled data + targeted human corrections concentrated on reward-model-flagged hard/mislabeled samples.

papers.title: ReasoningBank: Scaling Agent Self-Evolving with Reasoning Memory
papers.one_liner: Distills generalizable reasoning strategies from an agent's self-judged successful AND failed trajectories into retrievable memory items, and adds memory-aware test-time scaling (MaTTS) to generate diverse experience.
papers.relation: Closest prior art to skill optimization — but it learns from SYNTHETIC self-generated rollouts labeled by SELF-judgment, never from real user logs; that self-judging is exactly the noise-handling weak point the user's real-log/AI-judge signal could strengthen.
papers.data_source: The agent's own rollout trajectories, self-judged for success/failure (no external ground truth).

papers.title: GEPA: Reflective Prompt Evolution Can Outperform Reinforcement Learning
papers.one_liner: Evolves prompts by reflecting in natural language over execution traces, outperforming RL baselines with far fewer rollouts by using language feedback as the optimization signal.
papers.relation: The reflective, training-free optimizer the user's method builds on — but its feedback is task reward from synthesized rollouts, with no real-log signal; a natural insertion point for real interaction feedback and AI-judge scores.
papers.data_source: On-policy rollouts plus natural-language reflection over the resulting execution traces.

papers.title: Agentic Context Engineering: Evolving Contexts for Self-Improving Language Models
papers.one_liner: Treats context as an incrementally-updated 'playbook' evolved by a Generator/Reflector/Curator loop from natural execution feedback, avoiding context collapse and needing no labeled data.
papers.relation: Directly optimizes a reusable TEXTUAL artifact (playbook ≈ skill) — the closest structural match to the user's target — but its feedback is self-generated execution signal on synthetic runs, not real user logs, leaving it exposed to self-confirmation.
papers.data_source: Natural execution feedback from the agent's own runs (success/failure of self-generated trajectories).

papers.title: Escaping the Self-Confirmation Trap: An Execute-Distill-Verify Paradigm for Agentic Experience Learning
papers.one_liner: Decouples Execute/Distill/Verify across separate agents so that wrong-but-self-consistent trajectories aren't stored as good experience, filtering memory via a third-party distiller and cross-agent consensus.
papers.relation: An explicit noise-handling mechanism for experience/skill learning that targets self-judged label noise — but it substitutes expensive multi-agent consensus for ground truth; real-log outcomes could serve as the cheaper external verifier the user has.
papers.data_source: Multiple agents' synthesized trajectories on the same tasks, with an independent distiller and a consensus-based verifier.

papers.title: Building Customer Support AI Agents at 100M-User Scale: An Evaluation-Driven Framework
papers.one_liner: Nubank's production framework combines context engineering, human-in-the-loop prompt refinement, LLM-judge evaluation with inter-rater agreement, and GEPA optimization; offline metrics predicted a +37pp NPS and +29pp self-service gain in production.
papers.relation: The clearest 2026 example of turning production agent logs into prompt/skill improvements via an AI judge — exactly the user's setting — but it is ad hoc/industrial, offers no principled fusion of real logs with synthesized on-policy rollouts, and does not address judge validity.
papers.data_source: Production customer-support deployments (real logs) + LLM-as-judge evaluations + human-refined prompts, optimized with GEPA.

gaps: No method jointly optimizes a reusable SKILL artifact (a SKILL.md-style procedural doc / experience-library entry — not model weights, not a monolithic prompt) from BOTH offline real user-agent logs (with AI-judge-approximated feedback) AND freshly synthesized on-policy rollouts. Today the two signals live in separate literatures (log-mining papers like 2407.10944/2410.13852 vs on-policy optimizers like GEPA/ACE/ReasoningBank); a framework that fuses, weights, and reconciles a noisy off-policy log signal with a clean on-policy simulated signal to edit skills is unclaimed.

Off-policy credit-assignment for text skills: real logs were produced by a DIFFERENT agent/prompt than the skill being optimized, so they are off-policy, while synthesized rollouts are on-policy but simulated. No importance-weighting / off-policy-correction analog (as in off-policy RL) exists for using off-policy real interaction logs to safely edit an on-policy skill, nor any theory for when a log is 'close enough' to the current skill's policy to be trusted.

Judge-validity-aware skill optimization: given AI judges are 'reliable but not valid' (2606.19544) and reward-hackable (2606.04923, 2411.02306), there is no skill optimizer that calibrates or cross-checks its AI-judge feedback against the sparse real implicit signals in logs (thumbs, rephrases, task abandonment) to detect when a proposed skill edit is gaming the judge rather than genuinely helping users.

Selective-verification routing for skill edits: RLTHF (2502.13417) targets which HUMAN LABELS to buy; no analog decides which SKILL EDITS to trust by measuring agreement between real-log implicit feedback and simulated-rollout AI-judge feedback, then routing only the disagreements to expensive verification (real users or humans) — a direct, cheap denoising loop the user's dual-signal setup enables.

Closing the loop on a log-grounded simulator: RealUserSim (2605.20204) grounds a user simulator in WildChat for evaluation only. Nobody co-optimizes a skill against a log-grounded simulator AND validates the resulting skill improvement on held-out real logs, so current on-policy skill optimizers risk overfitting to simulator artifacts — exactly the failure Lost in Simulation (2601.17087) documents.

Breaking the self-confirmation trap with real-log ground truth instead of multi-agent consensus: EDV (2606.24428) needs several agents + a distiller + consensus to stop wrong-but-self-consistent trajectories from entering memory. An open, cheaper alternative is to use naturally occurring outcome signals in real logs (did the user's real follow-up indicate success?) as the external verifier for single-agent skill distillation.

Anti-manipulation guarantees for feedback-optimized skills: since feedback-maximization provably breeds targeted manipulation and sycophancy (2411.02306), there is no method that audits or constrains a learned skill so improvements are attributable to verifiable task success (from on-policy rollouts with a checkable reward) rather than to feedback-gaming detectable in the logs — a safety-critical gap specific to skills, which are reusable and transferable.

Turn-to-skill credit assignment under coarse, noisy signals: log feedback is conversation/turn-level and noisy (2507.23158), yet a skill library has many procedures. No method attributes a coarse implicit signal down to WHICH line/procedure of a multi-skill library caused the good or bad outcome, which is prerequisite to editing the right skill rather than the whole library.

Modeling missing-not-at-random censoring in production logs: explicit feedback is 1-3% sparse and delayed (RLUF 2505.14946), and silent-accept vs churned users leave no trace, so a naive log-reward is selection-biased. No skill-optimization method models this censoring/attrition when treating logs as reward — a survival-analysis-flavored opportunity.

Missing benchmark: 'real logs + replayable environment + ground-truth verifier.' tau-bench (2406.12045) has verifiers but synthetic users; WildChat (2405.01470) has real users but no environment to re-roll in. A benchmark that pairs real user-agent logs with a re-simulatable environment — so a skill optimized from logs can be re-rolled on-policy and scored by BOTH an AI-judge and a ground-truth verifier — is an open deliverable that would let the field measure exactly the log-plus-rollout fusion the user proposes.
